sourceMovie.title: Hummingbird
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Hummingbird works as a action movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The action and thriller blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: John Wick
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: John Wick (2014) captures the action appeal of Hummingbird without simply imitating it. The thriller streak running through both films is what puts John Wick at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: RocknRolla
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The action appeal of Hummingbird — that dark edge, the thriller — translates cleanly into RocknRolla (2008). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: A Working Man
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind A Working Man (2025) clearly understood what makes action work — the same quality that keeps Hummingbird rewatchable. Expect the same dark rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: The Dead Pool
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Dead Pool (1988) is the kind of film Hummingbird fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The action overlap is real, but it's the dark emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Year of the Dragon
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Year of the Dragon shares Hummingbird's gift for action — the 1985 film delivers thriller without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Cleaner
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Hummingbird compelling — the action, the thriller — shows up in Cleaner (2025) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that tense storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Constantine
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The action energy of Hummingbird is alive in Constantine (2005), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a dark payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Layer Cake
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Layer Cake (2004) operates in the same thriller as Hummingbird, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The dark payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: The Ghost and the Darkness
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Ghost and the Darkness (1996) belongs to the same conversation as Hummingbird — both anchor their thriller in adventure emotion that doesn't let go. The tense undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Dirty Pretty Things
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Hummingbird's thriller stayed with you, Dirty Pretty Things (2002) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The crime through-line is the real common thread between them.

faq.question: What should I watch after Hummingbird?
faq.answer: Try John Wick, RocknRolla, A Working Man—each shares DNA with Hummingbird while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Hummingbird?
faq.answer: Hummingbird (2013) is best described as Action, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Hummingbird worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 6.2/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this action lane.
